edge: Language models use reasoning to generate better answers by chaining accurate local inferences to estimate relationships between variables.
Chaining accurate local inferences enables estimation of relationships between variables not seen together in training.
Reasoning through intermediate variables reduces bias and is effective when training data consists of overlapping local clusters of variables.

The Bayes net has random variables {Yi}N i=1. The distribution defined by the Bayes net is denoted as pd, the data distribution.
The training data is a sequence of variable indices i ∈ {1, . . . N}, and variable values vi ∈ X. Variable values always follow variable indices.
We create local structure in our task via an observation distribution pobs, which is a distribution over subsets of the variable indices.
pd and pobs define a distribution p on training sequences, allowing the learner to see samples from the joint distribution over certain subsets of random variables.

Given the model q, we can use one of three different estimators for the conditional probabilities.
The direct prediction estimator is the baseline without any reasoning, directly outputting the probability estimated by q.

The scaffolded generation estimator represents ideal reasoning if we know the best set of steps to work through, using a scaffold set S consisting of variables to collectively d-separate the observed variable from the target variable.
Variables are ordered by their distance from the observed variable in the Bayes net, increasing.
In the case of a chain, the scaffold consists of all variables between Yi and Yj.
We estimate each variable given the observed variable and previously generated scaffold variables.
We approximately marginalize over the scaffold variables’ values using M Monte Carlo samples from the conditional distributions.
It is similar to scaffolded generation in that it generates intermediate variables before the target.
Free generation uses the model to choose which variables to instantiate in addition to estimating their values.
We simply sample variable indices and values from q until it generates the index of the target variable.
At that point, we compute the probability of the target variable.
